steps.title: Restate the hypothesis
steps.content: Gimsa and colleagues claim the sail improved success in "underwater pursuits of prey species capable of making quick, evasive movements." Catching evasive prey underwater depends on maneuverability — the ability to change direction quickly.

steps.title: Predict the supporting result
steps.content: A finding that the sail model maneuvers better underwater than the no-sail model — for example, completing sharp turns faster — would directly back the hypothesis.

steps.title: Eliminate per choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'sail... significantly less time to complete a sharp turn': faster sharp turns is exactly the maneuverability advantage the hypothesis predicts. ✓
  • Choice B — 'displaced significantly more water': about volume, not pursuit ability — irrelevant to chasing evasive prey. ✗
  • Choice C — 'significantly less battery power remaining': shows the sail costs more energy, which weakens rather than supports the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice D — 'took significantly longer to travel a specified distance': slower travel would undercut, not support, success in pursuits. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
